texts.label: section_header
texts.orig: Analogy and duality between random channel coding and lossy source coding
texts.text: Analogy and duality between random channel coding and lossy source coding

texts.label: text
texts.orig: Sergey Tridenski and Ram Zamir
texts.text: Sergey Tridenski and Ram Zamir

texts.label: text
texts.orig: Here we write in a unified fashion (using ' R ( P, Q, D ) ' [1]) the random coding exponents in channel coding and lossy source coding. 1 We derive their explicit forms and show, that, for a given random codebook distribution Q , the channel decoding error exponent can be viewed as an encoding success exponent in lossy source coding, and the channel correct-decoding exponent can be viewed as an encoding failure exponent in lossy source coding. We then extend the channel exponents to arbitrary D , which corresponds for D > 0 to erasure decoding and for D < 0 to list decoding. For comparison, we also derive the exact random coding exponent for Forney's optimum tradeoff decoder [2].
texts.text: Here we write in a unified fashion (using ' R ( P, Q, D ) ' [1]) the random coding exponents in channel coding and lossy source coding. 1 We derive their explicit forms and show, that, for a given random codebook distribution Q , the channel decoding error exponent can be viewed as an encoding success exponent in lossy source coding, and the channel correct-decoding exponent can be viewed as an encoding failure exponent in lossy source coding. We then extend the channel exponents to arbitrary D , which corresponds for D > 0 to erasure decoding and for D < 0 to list decoding. For comparison, we also derive the exact random coding exponent for Forney's optimum tradeoff decoder [2].

texts.label: text
texts.orig: In the case of source coding, we assume discrete memoryless sources with a finite alphabet X and a finite reproduction alphabet ˆ X . In the case of channel coding, we assume discrete memoryless channels with finite input and output alphabets X and Y , such that for any ( x, y ) ∈ X ×Y the channel probability is positive P ( y | x ) > 0 . For simplicity, let R denote an exponential size of a random codebook, such that there exist block lengths n for which e nR is integer. We assume the size of the codebook M = e nR for source coding, and M = e nR + 1 for channel coding. Let Q denote the (i.i.d.) distribution, according to which the codebook is generated. texts.orig: In order to combine the lower and upper bounds given by Theorem 10 and Theorem 11, and determine the true exponent of successful encoding, observe, that the lower bound (134) of Theorem 10 can be rewritten, analogously to (108), as the RHS of (2). As we have shown previously, the implicit expression on the RHS of (2) equals the explicit expression (18). As can be seen from (18), it is a convex ( ∪ ) function of ( R, D ) , and therefore it is continuous in ( R, D ) , except for the points where its result switches to the value + ∞ . This occurs for D = D min = min x, ˆ x d ( x, ˆ x ) . For this value of D , the upper bound of Theorem 11 is + ∞ , while the lower bound, given by Theorem 10, is finite. For all other values of D the bounds of Theorem 10 and Theorem 11 coincide. Therefore we have proved Theorem 1.
